title: The Master and Margarita
description: A satirical fantasy that collides Soviet literary bureaucracy with the demonic and the sacred.
descriptionParagraphs: A satirical fantasy that collides Soviet literary bureaucracy with the demonic and the sacred.

The Master and Margarita is usually read through its treatment of satire, religion, and state control. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes satire, religion, and state control feel immediate.

cover.alt: The Master and Margarita cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A satirical fantasy that collides Soviet literary bureaucracy with the demonic and the sacred.
banningParagraphs: The Master and Margarita entered censorship debates as a novel associated with satire, religion, and state control. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political dissent and religious content.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1930s-1960s in Soviet Union, where Soviet censors suppressed manuscript and publication. The novel's mockery of official culture and its religious imagination made it difficult to publish uncut. It circulated in incomplete or delayed form before becoming a classic.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.
